reviewed_items.reviewer_notes: An attacker can trigger this via P2P gossip by sending a valid DataColumnSidecar A, then sending a forged DataColumnSidecar M with the same inclusion proof and block header but malicious KzgCommitments. The cache key at data_column.go:527-547 includes only the 4-branch inclusion proof (128 bytes) and the block header hash (32 bytes), omitting the KzgCommitments being verified. When M arrives, inclusionProofKey() generates the same cache key, causing line 368-369 to skip full Merkle verification and accept M. The KZG proof verification at line 148 of validate_data_column.go then verifies M's cell data against M's malicious commitments (which the attacker crafted to match), but the critical inclusion proof—which should prove these commitments are actually in the block body—was bypassed. This violates the invariant from 01e_PARTIAL_W2B3_1771748669.json line 1060-1076: "Any cached/optimized inclusion proof verification must match full Merkle branch verification." Impact: attacker can cause nodes to accept and propagate data columns with arbitrary commitments not present in the actual block, breaking PeerDAS data availability guarantees and potentially causing network-wide acceptance of invalid block data. Severity is Critical per BUG_BOUNTY_SCOPE.json criteria: a single malicious P2P packet can affect the entire network by corrupting data availability sampling for all nodes (>50% impact on Prysm's 31% network share alone, plus cross-client propagation).
reviewed_items.spec_reference: 01e_PARTIAL_W2B3_1771748669.json PROP-6a4369e9-inv-042: "Any cached/optimized inclusion proof verification must match full Merkle branch verification. forall proof: optimized_merkle_verify(proof) == is_valid_merkle_branch(proof)"
